findings.suggestion: Use a parameterized query and let the driver bind the value: `db.execute("SELECT id, name FROM products WHERE name LIKE ?", (f"%{name}%",))`. Never build SQL with f-strings/concatenation from caller input.
findings.summary: Line 8: `db.execute(f"SELECT id, name FROM products WHERE name LIKE '%{name}%'")` interpolates the untrusted `name` argument directly into the SQL text. A value such as `x' UNION SELECT password FROM users--` closes the string literal and appends attacker-controlled SQL, allowing data exfiltration or destructive statements. The function's own comment (lines 6-7) confirms the intent to expose this flaw.
findings.title: SQL injection via f-string interpolation in search_products

nodes.label: Prior commits, decisions, agents, and command evidence

nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (trivial) completed.

New file source.py adds search_products, which builds a SQL query by f-string-interpolating the untrusted `name` argument (line 8) — a classic, directly-exploitable SQL injection. The inline comment accurately describes the defect, but the code does not enforce the safe contract a DB helper implies; the stated-intent-vs-implementation gap is resolved by the code actually being vulnerable. No callers exist yet (newly added), so no harm is realized, but any future caller inherits the hole. Fix is a standard sqlite3 parameterized query.

A single new 9-line file adds `search_products`, which contains a clear, unmitigated SQL injection: the `name` parameter is interpolated into the query via an f-string on line 8. This is a critical, confirmed vulnerability (the code comment itself documents it). No callers exist yet (blast radius 0), but the contract of the function is inherently unsafe and must be fixed before any use. Verification gate note: this is a static-analysis certainty — the injection is provable from the source line alone and needs no runtime artifact — so the missing-fresh-verification lead is resolved for this finding by code inspection.
